name: Austin Safe & Sound
tagline: Safety is infrastructure. Compassion is strategy.
mission:
We advocate for evidence-based public safety in Austin — where safety means both safe public spaces for all residents and dignified, effective pathways for people in crisis. We reject the false choice between enforcement and compassion. We track outcomes, not rhetoric.

Safety is infrastructure — it's a prerequisite for everything else. You can't have thriving neighborhoods, successful transit, or family-friendly communities if people don't feel safe. The framing is dual-track and technocratic, NOT culture war:

• Some unhoused people primarily need housing and a stable environment. For them, the Housing First model works. Housing IS the intervention.

• Some unhoused people are in mental health or substance abuse crises that prevent them from maintaining housing even when offered. For them, treatment must come first or alongside housing. Offering an apartment to someone in active psychosis isn't compassionate — it's inadequate.

• Different populations need different interventions. A single ideological frame (whether "just house them" OR "just enforce the law") is guaranteed to fail because it treats a heterogeneous population as homogeneous.

• Public safety and compassion are not in tension. Residents deserve safe public spaces AND unhoused people deserve dignity and pathways to stability. Anyone who tells you these are in conflict is either selling you something or hasn't thought it through.

Austin has been through the full political cycle on this issue. In 2019, Council repealed the camping ban, allowing visible encampments. In 2021, voters passed Prop B reinstating the ban (57-43%), reflecting widespread frustration across the political spectrum. The HEAL Initiative launched — compassionately closing highest-risk encampments by offering shelter pathways, moving 635+ people from encampments to shelters since launch. By 2024, shelter capacity doubled from 708 beds to 1,657 beds, and 600+ permanent supportive housing units were added. But outcomes are mixed: HEAL data shows 48% of people who exited shelters returned to homelessness. The Marshalling Yard shelter (300 beds, 18% of total capacity) improved its successful exit rate from 15% to 30% — better, but still means 70% don't exit to permanent housing.

In February 2026, the Austin Homeless Strategy Office published its first-ever strategic plan (2025-2027) with five focus areas: system leadership, impactful investments, crisis response, health/housing exits, and community engagement. The HSO identified $101M in additional funding needs. This is the moment to establish accountability.

Austin PD is significantly understaffed relative to population — this is a well-documented, ongoing crisis. The pragmatic position is BOTH more officers (especially patrol and community policing) AND more non-police crisis response (mental health teams, HOST initiative expansion). These are complements, not substitutes. A police officer should not be the primary responder to a mental health crisis, AND there need to be enough officers to handle actual crime. The funding for both needs to come from somewhere, which connects to the fiscal accountability theme.
theory_of_change: Accountability creates pressure for improvement. When someone is watching and publicly reporting on public safety outcomes, performance improves. Austin has made significant investments in homelessness services and policing, but outcome data is fragmented, inconsistent, and often invisible to the public. We fill the accountability gap by tracking what was promised against what was delivered — across the HSO strategic plan, shelter operations, APD staffing, and HEAL encampment management. Small organized groups showing up at council with specific data and specific asks change the conversation from ideology to evidence. We make the data visible, make the tradeoffs explicit, and create political incentives for evidence-based decisions rather than performative ones.

political_positioning.description: Wide compass cloud — public safety concern is genuinely universal. This org's coalition overlaps significantly with Texas and GOP clouds (everyone wants safe neighborhoods) while also overlapping with Democratic clouds (compassionate approach, investment in social services). The overlap is the point. We reject both the abolish-the-police left and the enforcement-only right. Center-right economically on policing (fund adequate staffing), center-left on social services (invest in crisis response and shelter). Pragmatic on all of it. Broad coalition: neighborhood associations, small business owners, service providers, urbanists, parents, and anyone who wants evidence rather than ideology driving public safety policy.

key_policy_positions: Hold the HSO accountable for measurable outcomes in its 2025-2027 strategic plan across all five focus areas
Track shelter bed utilization, wait times, exit-to-housing rates, and return-to-homelessness rates by facility
Monitor APD staffing levels and 911 response times by district — make the data public and neighborhood-specific
Demand HEAL initiative transparency: track encampment outcomes, not just activity counts
Support HOST team expansion as a complement to traditional policing, not a substitute
Advocate for adequate APD patrol and community policing staffing alongside accountability reforms
Push for public dashboards on homelessness spending and outcomes — disaggregate by population and intervention type
Apply dual-track framework: Housing First for those who can maintain housing, treatment-first for those in active crisis
Bridge public safety and homelessness policy — they are the same issue viewed from different angles
Ensure crisis response includes mental health and substance abuse intervention components alongside law enforcement

active_campaigns.title: HSO Strategic Plan Accountability
active_campaigns.summary:
MAJOR UPDATE (April 22, 2026): Multiple converging crises demand accountability scrutiny.

⚡ BUDGET CRISIS (April 16): FY2027 budget forecast projects $16.8M in social services cuts — directly targeting permanent supportive housing, workforce development, and adult education programs. Current year already cut $5.28M ($2.7M public health, $1.46M HSO). $26.4M projected deficit under no-new-revenue tax rate. THIS DIRECTLY THREATENS HSO STRATEGIC PLAN FUNDING. The $101M additional funding ask now faces a city in deficit. CONTEXT: Voters rejected Proposition Q last November, which would have generated $110M for parks and homelessness services — that loss is a major driver of the current fiscal squeeze. Bond package (BEATF initial recommendation: $750M, formal presentation to Council May 28) becomes the critical alternative funding mechanism.

⚡ TENANT PROTECTIONS (April 17): Council expanding eviction prevention at affordable rental housing. City redirecting rent assistance from direct payments to legal advocacy and negotiated settlements — rent assistance portal will NOT reopen. HousingWorks Austin emphasizing early intervention to prevent evictions.

APRIL 13: AHSO launched the operator solicitation for the South Austin Housing Navigation Center — the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center. Up to $250,000 in funding available. Expected to open late summer/early fall 2026.

⚡ ENCAMPMENT EXPANSION (April 16-17): AHSO dramatically expanding sweeps this summer. 42 city staff across six full-time HEM teams. Each team includes TWO APD officers. KEY CHANGE: outreach workers will NO LONGER be embedded in sweep teams — fundamental departure from HEAL model's services-first approach. 700+ 311 calls/month. Unhoused residents report losing all possessions. Advocates call it criminalizing homelessness. Previous sweeps cost $800,000+ with minimal housing outcomes. City acknowledges not enough shelters. HSO Director Gray will present the sweeps expansion plan to council at the MAY 5 work session. THIS IS THE ACCOUNTABILITY FLASHPOINT.

Strategic plan commitments (adopted March 26):
• 650 new shelter beds planned + 2 new housing navigation centers (first one now in procurement)
• $101M in proposed additional funding — NOW THREATENED by $16.8M social services cuts
• ~6,500 people currently on streets
• Endeavors operates ALL major shelters under emergency contract through September 2026
• Competitive solicitation for shelter operations starts October 1, 2026
• Potential bond project to replace Marshalling Yard with purpose-built facility
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Strategic plans without accountability mechanisms become performative documents. The HSO plan is the city's first-ever comprehensive homelessness strategy — if it succeeds, it's a model. If it fails quietly, Austin loses years. Public tracking with quarterly reports creates three pressure points: (1) city staff know someone is watching, (2) council members have data for budget decisions, (3) residents can see whether their tax dollars are producing results. This is the most leveraged campaign because it covers all homelessness spending and strategy, not just individual programs.
next_actions: Obtain and analyze the complete HSO 2025-2027 strategic plan — identify every measurable commitment and timeline
Build a public tracking dashboard mapping each of the five focus areas to specific metrics and deadlines
Attend HSO community engagement sessions and council briefings — establish presence as consistent accountability voice
Submit public records requests for baseline data on each strategic plan metric
Publish first quarterly progress report by Q2 2026
Identify which of the $101M funding gaps have been addressed and which remain
Present accountability findings at council budget hearings to inform FY2027 appropriations

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of HSO Strategic Plan Accountability often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If HSO Strategic Plan Accountability is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move HSO Strategic Plan Accountability forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): Major shelter operator consolidation — Endeavors now runs ALL major shelters: Marshalling Yard, Northbridge, ARCH, and Eighth Street downtown shelters under emergency contract through September 2026. Competitive solicitation for operations planned October 1, 2026. Director Gray has proposed a bond project to replace the Marshalling Yard with a new purpose-built shelter, potentially consolidating ARCH and Eighth Street shelters as well.

Austin doubled shelter beds to 1,657 but the Marshalling Yard exit rate is still only 30% — meaning 70% of residents don't exit to permanent housing. With the new strategic plan calling for 650 additional beds, the question shifts: will more beds with the same programming produce better outcomes, or does the model need to change? HEAL data shows 48% of people who exited shelters returned to homelessness — that's a revolving door.

Track bed utilization, wait times, exit rates, and return-to-homelessness rates by facility — now all under one operator (Endeavors), which should make data comparison easier. Disaggregate by population type. Identify which programs produce the best outcomes and advocate for scaling those models.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Shelter is the most visible and politically charged piece of homelessness infrastructure. Currently, the public debate is about bed counts — how many beds exist. But bed counts without outcome data are meaningless if 70% of residents cycle back to homelessness. Shifting the conversation from capacity to outcomes changes what success looks like and creates pressure to fund evidence-based programs. Facility-level data identifies what's working (scale it) and what's not (fix it or reallocate). Disaggregating by population type (economic crisis vs. chronic illness vs. substance abuse) enables the dual-track approach in practice.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for bed utilization rates, length of stay, and exit outcomes at each major shelter facility
Partner with ECHO for HMIS data on return-to-homelessness rates by shelter and program type
Research Marshalling Yard outcomes in detail — what changed to improve exit rates from 15% to 30%, and what would get to 50%
Build facility-level outcomes dashboard: utilization, exit-to-housing rate, return rate, average length of stay
Identify which shelter programs serve which populations — disaggregate outcomes by population type
Publish first shelter outcomes report comparing facility performance
Present findings to council with specific recommendations for scaling high-performing programs

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

active_campaigns.title: APD Staffing & Response Time Monitoring
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 22, 2026): APD staffing shows incremental progress but the 19% vacancy rate persists. Key numbers:

• 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate)
• FY2026 budget: $540M (36.2% of city's $1.5B general fund)
• New $218M APA contract provides 28% pay raise over 5 years
• Three cadet classes graduated since last spring — 122 total new officers
• 156th cadet class: 51 cadets, graduating August 2026
• ⚡ 157th class BEGAN YESTERDAY (APRIL 21) with 65 cadets in the pipeline — scaling toward 100-person classes
• Attrition slowing: net gain of 3 officers in 2025 (first positive year in 5-6 years)
• Recruitment interest up 166% per Chief Davis; Chief says fully staffed by end of 2027
• APA President Bullock: 'still about 700 officers short' when accounting for population growth

POSITIVE SIGNALS:
• For the first time since August 2023, APD met its target response time of 10 minutes 44 seconds or better for Priority 0 and Priority 1 calls.
• Integral Care is hiring 23 more mental health specialists for 24/7 crisis response.

BUDGET RISK: FY2027 forecast projects $26.4M deficit and $16.8M social services cuts. While APD's $540M budget is the largest single allocation, fiscal pressure could affect staffing pipeline funding. Monitor whether budget constraints slow the path to 100-person cadet classes.

NOTE: The HEM team expansion (see HSO campaign) means two APD officers per encampment team — 12 officers total diverted to sweep operations. With 340 vacancies, monitor whether this pulls patrol resources and affects response times. The 'both/and' model only works if officer allocation reflects priorities.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Response time data by district makes the staffing crisis concrete and neighborhood-specific rather than abstract and citywide. Council members cannot ignore data showing their district has deteriorating response times — it's the kind of constituent concern that drives budget votes. Simultaneously, tracking HOST and mental health team diversion rates demonstrates the value of non-police response: every mental health call handled by HOST is a call that doesn't require a patrol officer. This creates evidence for funding BOTH more officers AND more crisis teams, breaking the false binary. District-level data also reveals equity issues — which neighborhoods bear the worst of the staffing crisis.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for APD sworn officer counts, vacancy rates, and cadet class sizes over the past 3 years
Obtain 911 response time data by district and priority level — identify neighborhoods with worst response times
Research HOST team capacity: how many calls diverted from patrol, what outcomes, what expansion plans
Track mental health crisis response: how many calls, who responds, what outcomes compared to patrol response
Build district-level APD staffing and response time dashboard
Publish first APD staffing report with district-level response time analysis
Present response time data at council budget hearings — connect staffing levels to constituent safety outcomes

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of APD Staffing & Response Time Monitoring often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

active_campaigns.title: HEAL Initiative Transparency
active_campaigns.summary:
HEAL (Homeless Encampment Assistance Link) has been the primary mechanism for encampment management in Austin since 2021, moving 635+ people from encampments to shelters. But transparency on outcomes is limited. Track which encampments are targeted, what services are offered, how many people accept shelter, how many achieve permanent housing, and how many return to homelessness.

This is accountability work on an initiative that both sides of the debate should want to be transparent. Advocates for unhoused people want to ensure HEAL is actually connecting people to services, not just displacing them. Residents who want encampments addressed want to know that HEAL operations produce lasting results, not just temporary relocations. Whether the city is meeting its own stated criteria for 'compassionate' closure should be publicly verifiable.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: HEAL operates as the city's primary encampment intervention but with limited public outcome reporting. The initiative reports activity counts (encampments closed, people contacted) but not long-term outcomes (permanent housing placement, return to homelessness). Sunshine is the best disinfectant — publishing what actually happens during and after HEAL operations forces the program to demonstrate lasting results. This accountability serves both political flanks: those who support encampment management get evidence it works, those who oppose displacement get evidence of whether alternatives are offered. Transparent data de-polarizes the debate by replacing ideology with outcomes.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for HEAL operation data: encampments targeted, people contacted, services offered, shelter placements
Track post-HEAL outcomes: of people who accepted shelter, how many achieved permanent housing within 6 and 12 months
Attend HEAL community notification meetings to understand the process and document offers made
Research whether HEAL operations meet the city's own stated criteria for compassionate encampment closure
Compare HEAL outcomes across different encampment types and locations
Publish HEAL transparency report with operation-level outcome data

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of HEAL Initiative Transparency often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

aligned_organizations.name: Integral Care
aligned_organizations.relationship: Travis County's mental health authority and key partner on crisis response. Essential data source for understanding non-police intervention outcomes and mental health crisis response capacity. Their expansion directly reduces demand on APD for mental health calls.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Area Urban League
aligned_organizations.relationship: Operates Southbridge shelter. Aligned on equity dimensions of public safety and homelessness policy. Community credibility in East Austin communities disproportionately affected by both crime and homelessness.

aligned_organizations.name: Family Endeavors
aligned_organizations.relationship: Major homelessness service provider operating the Marshalling Yard and Northbridge shelters. Essential data partner for shelter outcomes tracking. Their program outcomes are central to our shelter accountability campaign.

aligned_organizations.name: ECHO
aligned_organizations.relationship: Ending Community Homelessness Coalition — administers the Homeless Management Information System (HMIS) and Point-in-Time count. Essential data and coordination partner for all homelessness outcome tracking.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Monitor
aligned_organizations.relationship: Independent local news source covering city government. Key information source for tracking council actions on public safety budgets, HSO operations, and APD staffing decisions.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Bulldog
aligned_organizations.relationship: Investigative local journalism. Information source for government accountability on public safety spending and homelessness program outcomes.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin YIMBY Action
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness — every unit of housing built reduces the pressure that pushes people out. Their success at building housing directly reduces homelessness inflow. The YIMBY housing agenda and our safety accountability agenda are complementary.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Family instability is a major pathway into homelessness. Their work on childcare, parental support, and family housing prevents the crises that lead to people losing housing. Family formation infrastructure is homelessness prevention infrastructure.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, neighborhood association meetings, local news coverage of public safety and homelessness data. Publish accountability dashboards — shelter outcomes tracker, APD response time maps, HSO progress reports — for organic discovery and sharing. Partner with Austin Monitor and Austin Bulldog for data-driven coverage.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a public safety briefing. Read the shelter outcomes tracker. Check your district's APD response times. Review the HSO strategic plan progress report. Share your neighborhood's experience with the safety-services gap.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment at council on HSO strategic plan accountability. Testify at budget hearings for evidence-based public safety spending. Share district-level response time data with your council member. Attend HEAL community notification meetings. Contact council members about shelter funding and APD staffing.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the data team tracking APD staffing and response times. Lead shelter outcomes research and facility-level analysis. Coordinate with Integral Care and ECHO on crisis response data. Represent us at HSO strategic plan review sessions. Present accountability findings at council hearings.

immediate.action: Obtain and analyze the complete HSO 2025-2027 strategic plan — identify every measurable commitment
Submit public records requests for APD staffing data, response times, and HEAL operation outcomes
Contact ECHO to establish data sharing partnership for HMIS shelter outcome data
Contact Austin YIMBY Action and Austin Abundance Project to formalize cross-org coordination

medium_term.action: Build and publish HSO strategic plan tracking dashboard with quarterly metrics
Publish first shelter outcomes report with facility-level data and population disaggregation
Build district-level APD staffing and response time dashboard
Publish first HEAL transparency report with operation-level outcome data

strategic.action: Present comprehensive HSO accountability report at council budget hearings for FY2027
strategic.notes: First-year accountability report covering all five HSO focus areas. Timed to influence FY2027 budget decisions on the $101M identified funding gap. This is the credibility-building moment — demonstrate that rigorous, nonpartisan accountability improves public safety outcomes.

strategic.action: Publish annual public safety outcomes report integrating all four campaign data streams
strategic.notes: Combine HSO progress, shelter outcomes, APD staffing, and HEAL data into a single annual report. The integrated view shows how these issues connect — staffing affects response times, shelter outcomes affect encampment populations, HEAL operations depend on shelter capacity. Make the system visible.

strategic.action: Advocate for permanent public dashboards on homelessness spending and public safety outcomes
strategic.notes: Use the org's tracking infrastructure as a proof of concept for what the city should publish itself. The goal is to make our accountability work unnecessary by getting the city to publish this data proactively. If we succeed, we've institutionalized transparency.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Residents understandably fear that reform rhetoric can translate into weaker enforcement, slower emergency response, or unclear accountability for homelessness programs, and they want clear proof that public safety spending is producing measurable results.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Accountability is pro-safety: faster response times and effective interventions require transparent metrics.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Performance dashboards and outcome tracking improve resource allocation and public trust across agencies.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can support first responders and still insist on clear goals, timelines, and outcomes."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Data transparency protects rights while improving service quality.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Publishing disaggregated outcomes helps identify disparities and correct harmful practices.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Better data is how we reduce harm and improve effectiveness at the same time."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Measure what works before expanding programs.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Program evaluations can redirect spending from low-impact interventions to high-impact safety and housing supports.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If taxpayers fund a program, taxpayers deserve to know what outcomes they bought."

common_ground: People deserve to feel safe in every neighborhood.
Emergency response should be fast, fair, and reliable.
Taxpayer-funded programs should publish measurable outcomes.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Do not label all criticism as anti-reform or anti-police.
Do not reduce complex homelessness issues to a single agency failure.
Do not overclaim causality from short-term trend changes.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Attribution of year-to-year crime changes to any single intervention.
Long-term outcomes for newer homelessness initiatives.
Staffing pipelines affected by national labor-market conditions.
honest_limits: We do not run city departments directly.
We cannot guarantee immediate citywide response-time improvements.
We cannot solve homelessness without county, state, and nonprofit partners.
